items.q: Which type of muscle is under voluntary control?
items.why: Students think all muscles can be controlled if we try hard enough. Cardiac and smooth muscle are involuntary - you can't consciously beat your heart. Only skeletal muscle responds to conscious commands.

items.q: What does the 'I' in FITT stand for?
items.why: Students guess. FITT = Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type - the variables you adjust to apply progressive overload. Intensity = how hard (e.g., % of 1RM or heart rate).

items.correct: Increases red blood cell production, improving oxygen-carrying capacity.
items.why: Students assume all PEDs work the same way. EPO is specifically for endurance - more red cells = more oxygen to working muscles. Anabolic steroids do something different (promote protein synthesis).
The answer examiners want is: Ball and socket. “Hinge” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Muscle to bone. “Bone to bone” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many main joint types were listed (hinge, ball-and-socket, pivot)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
